title: Restoring Law and Order Act of 2025
short_title: Restoring Law and Order Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Restoring Law and Order Act of 2025 creates a program that gives 500 million dollars to police departments across the country. This money helps police hire more officers and fight different types of crimes like stealing cars, selling drugs, and child trafficking. The law also helps keep criminals who commit crimes over and over again in jail for longer periods. The goal is to make communities safer by giving police more resources and tools to stop crime.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Restoring Law and Order Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Grant Program
sections.text: This section adds new rules to an existing law called the Omnibus Crime Control and Safe Streets Act of nineteen sixty eight. The new rules create a grant program called the Make America Safe Again Grant Program. Only certain groups can get these grants. These groups are called eligible entities and include state police agencies, local police departments, and tribal law enforcement that are legally allowed to prevent crime, investigate crimes, or prosecute criminals. The Attorney General will give money grants to these eligible entities for eight specific purposes. First, they can hire and keep more police officers, including giving bonuses to current officers. Second, they can target and fight car thefts, including carjackings where someone steals a car with the driver still inside. Third, they can prevent violent crime by making sure repeat criminals, including young offenders, get tough prison sentences. Fourth, they can use public safety tools like bail and keeping dangerous criminals in jail before trial to stop them from going back to their communities. Fifth, they can get resources to better fight drug crimes and crimes involving fentanyl, which is a deadly drug. Sixth, they can detain and deport people who are in the country illegally and have committed crimes in the United States. Seventh, they can eliminate backlogs of investigations and process criminal evidence faster. Eighth, they can combat child trafficking between states. To get a grant, an eligible entity must submit an application to the Attorney General at the time and in the format that the Attorney General requires. The Attorney General can create guidelines, rules, and procedures to run this program, including rules about how to submit and review grant applications. Any group that gets a grant must keep records as required by the Attorney General to help with audits about receiving the grant, using the grant money, or work they hire other companies to do. The Attorney General can access any book, document, or record of a grant recipient, the state or local government where they operate, or any company they hire with grant money if these records relate to receiving or using the grant. The funding for this program comes from taking back money that was set aside but not yet spent under a different law. Five hundred million dollars of this money will go to the Attorney General for fiscal year twenty twenty six to run this program, and this money will stay available until September thirtieth twenty thirty. The rest of the money goes back to the Treasury. Additionally, any money the Attorney General has for diversity, equity, or inclusion programs under Executive Order fourteen thousand thirty five must instead be used for this law enforcement grant program.
